title: Designing the storage layer for a mini DBMS
summary: Why I chose a slotted page format over fixed-size records, and what a page actually holds.
content:
This is the first build log in the **Mini DBMS** series. Before a database can do
anything interesting, it needs a way to put bytes on disk and find them again.

## The page is the unit of I/O

Everything starts with the *page* — a fixed-size block (4KB here) that the storage
engine reads and writes as a single unit. Records live inside pages.

A slotted page keeps a small directory of `(offset, length)` slots at the front and
grows the data from the back:

```c
typedef struct {
  uint16_t slot_count;
  uint16_t free_offset;
  Slot     slots[]; // grows forward; record bytes grow backward
} PageHeader;
```

> The wrong turn I took first: fixed-size records. They're simpler, but they waste
> space the moment your rows vary in length — which is always.

## What's next

In the next post I work through inserting into a B+ tree and the split that I got
wrong twice.

title: The B+ tree split I got wrong twice
summary: A build log on node splitting: the off-by-one in the median pick that cost me an afternoon.
content:
Inserting into a B+ tree is easy until a node fills up. Then you *split*, and the
split is where I lost an afternoon.

## The median pick

When a leaf overflows you push the median key up to the parent. My bug: I copied the
median into the right node **and** left it in the left node.

```python
mid = len(keys) // 2
left, right = keys[:mid], keys[mid:]  # median stays in `right`, promoted by copy
```

The fix was deciding, once, whether the median lives in the right node (leaf split)
or moves up entirely (internal split). Those are different rules and conflating them
is the classic mistake.

## Takeaways

- Leaf split: median is *copied* up, stays in the right leaf.
- Internal split: median *moves* up, lives in neither child.
